question: [File: 'File Name: rest/schedules.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: getSchedules()] Can an unprivileged external user send repeated GET /schedules requests with no filters, causing pool.queryQuietly() to execute schedulesMainQuery with defaultLimit rows each time, exhausting DB connection pool slots and degrading >= 30% of node processing capacity?

[File: 'File Name: rest/schedules.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: getSchedules()] Can an unprivileged external user set limit to its maximum accepted value in acceptedSchedulesParameters, causing getSchedulesQuery() to return a massive result set that saturates DB I/O and degrades >= 30% of processing nodes?

[File: 'File Name: rest/schedules.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: getSchedules()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger three concurrent pool.queryQuietly() calls per request (schedulesQuery, entityQuery, signatureQuery) by sending many parallel requests, exhausting the DB connection pool and causing >= 30% node degradation?

[File: 'File Name: rest/schedules.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: getSchedules()] Can an unprivileged external user abuse Promise.all([pool.queryQuietly(entityQuery, entityIds), pool.queryQuietly(signatureQuery, entityIds)]) by requesting max-limit schedules, causing two simultaneous expensive DB queries per request that multiply connection usage and degrade >= 30% of nodes?

[File: 'File Name: rest/schedules.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: getSchedules()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a request that causes transactionSignatureJsonAgg to aggregate an unbounded number of signatures per schedule_id, causing the DB to perform excessive json_agg() work that degrades >= 30% of processing nodes?

[File: 'File Name: rest/schedules.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: getSchedules()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the signatureQuery's json_agg with ORDER BY ts.consensus_timestamp on a large transaction_signature table to cause a full table sort operation per request, degrading >= 30% of node processing capacity?

[File: 'File Name: rest/schedules.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: getSchedules()] Can an unprivileged external user send requests with account_id filter set to a value matching a creator_account_id with thousands of schedules, causing the entityQuery and signatureQuery to build large IN (${positions}) clauses that degrade DB performance across >= 30% of nodes?

[File: 'File Name: rest/schedules.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: getSchedules()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the range(1, entityIds.length + 1).map((i) => `$${i}`).join(',') construction to generate a very large parameterized IN clause in entityQuery and signatureQuery, causing DB query plan degradation that affects >= 30% of nodes?

[File: 'File Name: rest/schedules.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: getSchedules()] Can an unprivileged external user repeatedly paginate through all schedules using the next link generated by utils.getPaginationLink(), issuing continuous sequential requests that sustain high DB load and degrade >= 30% of processing nodes?

[File: 'File Name: rest/schedules.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: getScheduleById()] Can an unprivileged external user send a high volume of GET /schedules/:scheduleId requests for valid schedule IDs, causing pool.queryQuietly(getScheduleByIdQuery) to execute the four-table JOIN (schedule, entity, transaction_signature with json_agg) repeatedly and degrade >= 30% of node processing capacity?

[File: 'File Name: rest/schedules.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: getScheduleById()] Can an unprivileged external user target a schedule_id with a very large number of associated transaction_signature rows, causing the getScheduleByIdQuery's json_agg(... order by ts.consensus_timestamp) to perform an expensive sort and aggregation that degrades >= 30% of nodes?

[File: 'File Name: rest/schedules.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: getScheduleById()] Can an unprivileged external user enumerate valid scheduleId values and flood requests for schedules with large transaction_body blobs, causing pool.queryQuietly() to return large payloads that exhaust network bandwidth and degrade >= 30% of processing nodes?

[File: 'File Name: rest/schedules.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: getScheduleCacheControlHeader()] Can an unprivileged external user repeatedly request schedules that do not satisfy hasExecuted, hasAutoExpired, or hasExpired conditions (returning empty cache-control {}), bypassing caching and forcing every request to hit the DB, degrading >= 30% of processing nodes?

[File: 'File Name: rest/schedules.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: getScheduleCacheControlHeader()] Can an unprivileged external user identify active (non-expired, non-executed) schedules and flood requests for them, knowing the empty cache-control header {} means no CDN/proxy caching occurs, causing all requests to reach the DB and degrade >= 30% of nodes?

[File: 'File Name: rest/schedules.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: getScheduleCacheControlHeader()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the LONGER_SCHEDULE_CACHE_CONTROL_HEADER logic gap — where schedules with expirationTime=null and consensusTimestamp near the 31-minute boundary are not yet cached — to sustain a window of uncached high-frequency requests that degrade >= 30% of nodes?

[File: 'File Name: rest/schedules.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: extractSqlFromScheduleFilters()] Can an unprivileged external user supply both account_id and schedule_id filters simultaneously, causing extractSqlFromScheduleFilters() to build a compound WHERE clause that forces a multi-column index scan, increasing DB CPU usage and degrading >= 30% of processing nodes?

[File: 'File Name: rest/schedules.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: extractSqlFromScheduleFilters()] Can an unprivileged external user send requests with order=desc combined with a large limit, causing scheduleOrderQuery() to force a reverse index scan on s.schedule_id that is more expensive than forward scans, degrading >= 30% of nodes under concurrent load?

[File: 'File Name: rest/schedules.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: extractSqlFromScheduleFilters()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that filterColumnMap only maps account_id and schedule_id, meaning unknown filter keys are silently skipped (continue), allowing crafted requests with many unrecognized parameters to pass validation and still trigger full-table scans via schedulesMainQuery?

[File: 'File Name: rest/schedules.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: getSchedulesQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a request that results in getSchedulesQuery() producing a query with no WHERE clause (empty filterQuery), causing a full table scan of the schedule table on every request, and under concurrent load degrade >= 30% of processing nodes?

[File: 'File Name: rest/schedules.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: getSchedules()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of rate limiting on the /schedules endpoint to send thousands of requests per second, each triggering pool.queryQuietly(schedulesQuery) plus two additional queries, exhausting DB connections and degrading >= 30% of nodes?

[File: 'File Name: rest/schedules.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: getSchedules()] Can an unprivileged external user send concurrent requests with different account_id values, causing the DB to execute many parallel creator_account_id index lookups simultaneously, saturating DB CPU and degrading >= 30% of processing nodes?

[File: 'File Name: rest/schedules.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: mergeScheduleEntities()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger a large schedules response where mergeScheduleEntities() builds entityMap and signatureMap over thousands of entries, causing excessive JavaScript heap allocation in the mirror node process and degrading >= 30% of node processing capacity?

[File: 'File Name: rest/schedules.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: mergeScheduleEntities()] Can an unprivileged external user cause mergeScheduleEntities() to call Object.assign() on thousands of schedule objects simultaneously across many concurrent requests, causing GC pressure in the Node.js process and degrading >= 30% of processing nodes?

[File: 'File Name: rest/schedules.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: formatScheduleRow()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger responses where formatScheduleRow() calls row.signatures.filter().map() over a very large signatures array per schedule, causing high CPU usage in the mirror node process under concurrent load and degrading >= 30% of nodes?

[File: 'File Name: rest/schedules.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: formatScheduleRow()] Can an unprivileged external user request schedules with large transaction_body blobs, causing utils.encodeBase64(row.transaction_body) to perform expensive base64 encoding on every response, and under concurrent load degrade >= 30% of processing nodes?
